citation.publication_title: Tropical agroforestry supports insect pollinators and improves bean yield
citation.endnote_reference: Kingazi, N. (2024) Tropical agroforestry supports insect pollinators and improves bean yield. Journal of Applied Ecology. doi:10.1111/1365-2664.14629

abstract: 1. Pollination services contribute to crop productivity worldwide, but insect pollina- of Agriculture, Morogoro, Tanzania tors are declining in most agricultural landscapes, mainly due to agricultural ex- 2College of Forestry, Wildlife and Tourism, Sokoine University of Agriculture (SUA), pansion and an increase in intensive agricultural practices. To reduce the negative Morogoro, Tanzania effects of agricultural expansion and intensification, farmers can adopt ecologi- 3Tanzania Forestry Research Institute cal interventions, such as diversifying agricultural systems through agroforestry. (TAFORI), Morogoro, Tanzania 4Department of Ecology, Swedish However, there have been variable results on the effectiveness of agroforestry in University of Agricultural Sciences (SLU), enhancing pollinators and pollination services. Furthermore, most of the informa- Uppsala, Sweden tion has been generated in temperate regions, while the impact of agroforestry in 2. Using common beans (Phaseolus vulgaris L.) as the focal crop, this study tested whether a tropical agroforestry system called Chagga home gardens supports 3. The beans planted in the agroforestry plots had almost twice the abundance of insect pollinators, three times the richness of the species and almost twice the visitation rates than those planted in non- agroforestry plots. We also found a sig- nificant positive effect of woody cover in the surrounding landscape on insect pol- linator abundances, but not on species richness and visitation rates. Additionally, the abundance and richness of insect pollinators increased significantly with flower abundance, while the overall plant richness in a plot significantly increased insect pollinator abundance and their visitation rate. The difference in bean yield between unbagged flowers (in which insect pollinators were allowed to access bean flowers) and bagged flowers and the total yield were higher in agroforestry than in non- agroforestry plots. 4. Synthesis and applications. This study showed that, compared to monocul- ture, agroforestry generally promotes pollination services in a tropical context. Furthermore, we found that agroforestry is likely to be particularly helpful for © 2024 The Authors. Journal of Applied Ecology © 2024 British Ecological Society. | J Appl Ecol. 2024;61:1067-1080. wileyonlinelibrary.com/journal/jpe 1067 1068 | KINGAZI et al. pollinators when it increases flower abundance and plant richness and if it is co- ordinated so that woody cover also increases at the landscape scale
